what: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: Citation Tone Research Hub — interactive Shiny app accompanying R functions citation tone analysis across tone languages. Developed Chenzi Xu. Co-authored Cong Zhang. Shinytone integrates full citation tone analysis workflow one place: pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, growth curve analysis, generalised additive mixed models, outlier inspection, Chao tone numeral summarisation.

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: 1. Online app — install needed hosted version runs https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. Best small datasets, classroom demos, trying workflow without setup. 2. Local R package — full control Install GitHub: Launch UI offline (upload limits, memory caps, recordings stay machine): call analytical functions directly script RMarkdown document:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("chenchenzi/citationtone_hub") shinytone::run_app() library(shinytone) # Programmatic API will be exposed in subsequent releases: # df <- normalise_f0(df, method = "semitone", speaker = "speaker_id") # gca <- fit_gca(df, time = "time_norm", tone = "tone")

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: use Shinytone published work, please cite:
code: citation("shinytone")

title: shinytone — A Citation Tone Research Hub
text: MIT (code). Long-form tutorials vignettes CC -NC 4.0; see individual files details.

what: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: Returns short shape name ("level", "rising", "falling", "dipping", "peaking", finer "low level" / "mid level" / "high level" single-digit / repeated-digit strings).

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour(chao_str)

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: chao_str character string Chao digits (1-5), e.g. "55", "214", "35".

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
text: character string naming shape.

title: Classify a Chao tone numeral string as a shape — classify_contour
code: classify_contour("55")    # "high level" #> [1] "high level" classify_contour("214")   # "dipping" #> [1] "dipping" classify_contour("35")    # "rising" #> [1] "rising" classify_contour("51")    # "falling" #> [1] "falling"

what: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: Normalise time [0, 1] per token, bin 50 equally-spaced time points, average f0 across tokens tone. Returns long-format mean contour expected contour_to_chao().

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
code: compute_mean_contour(   data,   token = "token",   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   tone = "tone",   n_bins = 50 )

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: data Long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. token, f0, time, tone Column names. n_bins Number evenly-spaced time bins across [0, 1]. Default 50.

title: Compute the per-tone mean f0 contour from long-format data — compute_mean_contour
text: data frame columns tone, time, f0_predicted, one row per (tone, time-bin).

what: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: tone contour_data, sample contour turning points (endpoints turning point detected), map sample Chao digit (1-5) using three methods:

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
code: contour_to_chao(   contour_data,   tone_col = "tone",   time_col = "time",   f0_col = "f0_predicted",   threshold = 0.5,   raw_data = NULL,   raw_token = "token",   raw_f0 = "f0",   raw_tone = "tone" )

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: contour_data Per-tone contour data, typically compute_mean_contour(), predict_gca(), predict_gamm(). Must contain tone, time, f0_predicted columns (rename via tone_col, time_col, f0_col arguments). tone_col, time_col, f0_col Column names within contour_data. threshold Chao-units threshold turning-point detection. Default 0.5. raw_data Optional long-format raw data, used compute robust . NULL, robust column left NA. raw_token, raw_f0, raw_tone Column names raw_data.

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: data frame one row per tone, containing: tone — tone label n_digits — number digits numeral (2 3) refline — reference-line method numeral, e.g. "55" interval — interval-based numeral, e.g. "45" robust — robust numeral (NA raw_data given) shape — shape name classify_contour() applied refline sample_times, ref_continuous, int_continuous, rob_continuous — list columns per-sample numeric values

title: Convert per-tone contours to Chao tone numerals — contour_to_chao
text: Reference-line [1, 5] — f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 4 + 1, rounded. Interval-based (0, 5] — f0' = (f0 - f0_min) / (f0_max - f0_min) * 5, ceiling. Robust (0, 5] (optional) — formula range defined mu ± sigma per-token f0 peaks highest-pitched tone (max) per-token f0 valleys lowest-pitched tone (min). Computed raw_data supplied. turning-point detection uses Chao-units threshold (default 0.5) [1, 5] reference scale: tone gets 3 digits either dip peak exceeds threshold relative endpoints, otherwise 2 digits.

what: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: Convenience wrapper around mgcv::bam() citation-tone GAMM use case. Prepares data, builds smooth-based formula optional difference smooths speaker random smooths, fits model, can refit AR1 correlation requested.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
code: fit_gamm(   data,   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   token = "token",   tone = "tone",   speaker = "speaker",   item = "item",   duration = NULL,   k = 10,   bs = "tp",   smooth_type = c("separate", "difference"),   random_intercept_speaker = TRUE,   random_intercept_item = TRUE,   random_smooth = c("none", "speaker", "speaker_tone", "speaker_by_tone", "ref_diff"),   use_ar1 = FALSE )

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: data Long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0, time, token, tone, speaker, item Column names. duration Optional column name per-sample duration covariate. Use NULL omit duration smooth. k Basis dimension time smooth. Default 10. bs Spline basis type passed mgcv::s(). Default "tp". smooth_type Either "separate" (one smooth per tone via s(time, = tone)) "difference" (reference smooth + difference smooths via ordered factor). random_intercept_speaker, random_intercept_item Logical, include corresponding s(..., bs = "re") random intercept. random_smooth One "none", "speaker", "speaker_tone", "speaker_by_tone", "ref_diff". See Random smooth strategies details. use_ar1 Logical. TRUE, fit , estimate rho residuals' lag-1 autocorrelation, refit rho.

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: S3 object class "shinytone_gamm", list : model — fitted mgcv::bam() object formula_str — user-readable formula string rho — NULL AR1 rho value convergence_warning — NULL captured warning message smooth_type, random_smooth — passed (needed predict_gamm()) col_names — original column names, back-mapping

title: Fit a Generalised Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) to f0 contours — fit_gamm
text: non-standard model structures, use mgcv::bam() / mgcv::gam() directly. Random smooth strategies (Sóskuthy 2021): "speaker" — single -speaker factor smooth "speaker_tone" — interaction factor smooth speaker × tone "speaker_by_tone" — -speaker factor smooth, separately per tone "ref_diff" — reference -speaker smooth plus tone-difference smooths (matches smooth_type = "difference")

what: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: Convenience wrapper around lme4::lmer() citation-tone GCA use case. Prepares data (per-token time normalisation, orthogonal polynomial basis requested degree), builds conventional formula (ot1 + ... + otK) * tone + (random effects), fits model warning capture.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
code: fit_gca(   data,   f0 = "f0",   time = "time",   token = "token",   tone = "tone",   speaker = "speaker",   item = "item",   degree = 2,   random_intercept_speaker = TRUE,   random_intercept_item = TRUE,   random_slope_speaker = TRUE,   random_slope_item = FALSE )

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: data Long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0, time, token, tone, speaker, item Column names f0, time within token, token ID, tone category, speaker ID, item / word. degree Polynomial degree (1, 2, 3). Default 2. random_intercept_speaker, random_intercept_item Logical, include -speaker / -item random intercept. Default TRUE. random_slope_speaker, random_slope_item Logical, include -speaker / -item random slopes orthogonal-polynomial terms (ot1 .. otK). Default TRUE speaker, FALSE item.

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: S3 object class "shinytone_gca", list : model — fitted lme4::lmerMod object formula_str — user-readable formula string (original column names) poly_coefs — coefs stats::poly() needed predict_gca() degree — polynomial degree col_names — original column names (back-mapping summary tables) convergence_warning — NULL captured warning message

title: Fit a Growth Curve Analysis (GCA) model to f0 contours — fit_gca
text: non-standard model structures (custom contrasts, additional fixed effects, alternative RE structures), use lme4::lmer() directly.

what: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: token (unique value token column), normalise time axis [-1, 1], construct Legendre polynomial basis requested degree, fit ordinary least squares. Returns one row per token fitted coefficients c0, c1, ..., c{degree}.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
code: fit_polynomial(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   degree = 2 )

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: data Long-format data frame one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 values. Default "f0". Normalised f0 ("f0_st", "f0_zscore") recommended cross-speaker comparison. token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time within token. Default "time". speaker Column name speaker ID, carried output. Default "speaker". tone Column name tone category, carried output. Default "tone". degree Polynomial degree. One 1, 2, 3. Default 2.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: token-level data frame one row per token, containing: token, speaker, tone, coefficients c0, c1, ..., c{degree}.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
text: Legendre polynomials orthogonal [-1, 1], fitted coefficients tidy shape interpretation: c0 — token-level mean f0 c1 — linear slope across contour c2 — quadratic curvature (rise-fall vs fall-rise) c3 — cubic shape (sigmoid vs reverse-sigmoid) Tokens fewer degree + 1 valid samples receive NA coefficients. Tokens single unique time point keep c0 mean f0 set higher-order coefficients NA.

title: Fit Legendre polynomials to f0 contours, token by token — fit_polynomial
code: df <- data.frame(   token   = rep(c("t1", "t2"), each = 5),   time    = rep(seq(0, 1, length.out = 5), 2),   f0      = c(150, 160, 170, 165, 155,               140, 145, 150, 145, 140),   speaker = rep("S01", 10),   tone    = rep(c("T1", "T2"), each = 5) ) fit_polynomial(df, degree = 2) #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   token speaker tone     c0       c1     c2 #>   <chr> <chr>   <chr> <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 t1    S01     T1     163. 3.00e+ 0 -10.5  #> 2 t2    S01     T2     145. 9.15e-15  -5.71

what: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: token (unique value token column), compute maximum minimum f0. Within speaker, z-score per-token extremes. token flagged whenever absolute z-score either max min exceeds z_threshold.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
code: flag_outliers(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   speaker = "speaker",   z_threshold = 3 )

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: data long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 (Hz). Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". speaker Column name speaker ID. Default "speaker". z_threshold Absolute z-score token flagged. Default 3, covering 99.7% normal distribution.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
text: token-level data frame (one row per token) columns: f0_token_max, f0_token_min, f0_token_mean, f0_token_sd, z_max, z_min, flag_too_high, flag_too_low, plus original token speaker columns.

title: Flag per-token f0 outliers using by-speaker z-scores — flag_outliers
code: df <- data.frame(   token   = rep(c("t1", "t2", "t3"), each = 4),   speaker = rep("S01", 12),   f0      = c(150, 160, 155, 158,               152, 159, 154, 157,               400, 410, 405, 408)   # t3 is clearly a tracking error ) flag_outliers(df, z_threshold = 1.5) #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   token f0_token_max f0_token_min f0_token_mean f0_token_sd speaker  z_max #>   <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>         <dbl>       <dbl> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1 t1             160          150          156.        4.35 S01     -0.574 #> 2 t2             159          152          156.        3.11 S01     -0.581 #> 3 t3             410          400          406.        4.35 S01      1.15  #> # ℹ 3 more variables: z_min <dbl>, flag_too_high <lgl>, flag_too_low <lgl>

what: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Within token, compute semitone difference consecutive f0 samples. Flag samples rate change exceeds physiological plausibility (Sundberg 1973), Hz ratio crosses octave boundary, sample carryover upstream error (Steffman & Cole 2022).

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
code: flag_pitch_jumps(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   time_unit = c("s", "ms", "norm"),   rise_threshold = 1.263,   fall_threshold = 1.714,   octave_bounds = c(0.49, 1.99),   carryover_mult = 1.5 )

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: data long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 (Hz). Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time. Default "time". time_unit One "s", "ms", "norm". Default "s". "norm" option treats step one 10 ms-equivalent unit. rise_threshold Maximum plausible rise ST per 10 ms. Default 1.263 (Sundberg 1973). fall_threshold Maximum plausible fall ST per 10 ms. Default 1.714 (Sundberg 1973). octave_bounds Hz-ratio bounds outside step flagged octave jump. Default c(0.49, 1.99) (halving / doubling). carryover_mult Carryover band multiple rise/fall threshold (semitones). 0 disables carryover. Default 1.5.

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: input data frame two appended columns: flagged_jump (logical) jump_note (character; one "jump (rise)", "jump (fall)", "octave jump", "carryover", compound notes joined "; ").

title: Flag sample-to-sample f0 jumps within tokens — flag_pitch_jumps
text: Rise / fall thresholds expressed semitones per 10 ms; function rescales observed rate using time_unit compare . Zero-valued f0 samples (unvoiced markers pitch trackers) treated missing computation.

what: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: Wrapper runs flag_outliers() (-speaker z-score outlier detection per-token max / min) flag_pitch_jumps() (sample--sample jumps, octave jumps, carryover), combines results single long-format data frame one row per f0 sample.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
code: inspect_f0(   data,   f0 = "f0",   token = "token",   time = "time",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   z_threshold = 3,   rise_threshold = 1.263,   fall_threshold = 1.714,   octave_bounds = c(0.49, 1.99),   carryover_mult = 1.5,   time_unit = c("s", "ms", "norm") )

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: data long-format data frame, one row per f0 sample. f0 Column name f0 (Hz). Default "f0". token Column name token ID. Default "token". time Column name time. Default "time". speaker Column name speaker ID. Default "speaker". tone Column name tone category. Default "tone". z_threshold Absolute z-score token flagged. Default 3, covering 99.7% normal distribution. rise_threshold Maximum plausible rise ST per 10 ms. Default 1.263 (Sundberg 1973). fall_threshold Maximum plausible fall ST per 10 ms. Default 1.714 (Sundberg 1973). octave_bounds Hz-ratio bounds outside step flagged octave jump. Default c(0.49, 1.99) (halving / doubling). carryover_mult Carryover band multiple rise/fall threshold (semitones). 0 disables carryover. Default 1.5. time_unit One "s", "ms", "norm". Default "s". "norm" option treats step one 10 ms-equivalent unit.

title: Inspect f0 data for token-level outliers and sample-level jumps — inspect_f0
text: long-format data frame containing original token, time, f0, speaker, tone columns plus: f0_token_max, f0_token_min, f0_token_mean, f0_token_sd — per-token summaries flagged_jump — logical, sample-level jump flag flagged_token — logical, TRUE token extreme value sample-level jump flag_notes — human-readable concatenation reasons sample flagged

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Add normalised f0 column data, computed per speaker. Two methods available: semitones referenced per-speaker mean (perceptually uniform), per-speaker z-scores (statistical centring scaling).

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
code: normalise_f0(   data,   f0 = "f0",   speaker = "speaker",   tone = "tone",   method = c("semitone", "zscore"),   mean_method = c("weighted", "simple") )

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: data data frame containing least f0, speaker, tone columns. f0 Name column raw f0 values (Hz). Default "f0". speaker Name speaker ID column. Default "speaker". tone Name tone category column. Default "tone". Used mean_method = "weighted". method Normalisation formula. One : "semitone" (default): ST = 12 * log2(f0 / speaker_mean). "zscore": Z = (f0 - speaker_mean) / sd(f0_speaker). mean_method per-speaker mean computed. One "weighted" (default) "simple". See Details.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: input data frame two added columns: speaker_mean — per-speaker mean used normalisation f0_st (method = "semitone") f0_zscore (method = "zscore")

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0
text: Per-speaker means can computed two ways: "weighted" (default): mean per-tone means. tone contributes equally centre speaker's tonal space, regardless many tokens . Preferred unbalanced designs. "simple": arithmetic mean f0 observations speaker.

title: Normalise f0 by speaker — normalise_f0

what: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm
title: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm
text: Builds per-tone time grid, sets random-effect columns reference levels, predicts random-effect smooths excluded result population-average curve per tone.

title: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm
code: predict_gamm(gamm_obj, n = 200)

title: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm
text: gamm_obj object class "shinytone_gamm" fit_gamm(). n Number time points across [0, 1]. Default 200.

title: Predict population-level f0 smooth curves from a GAMM fit — predict_gamm
text: data frame columns time, f0_predicted, se, tone.

what: Predict population-level f0 contours from a GCA fit — predict_gca
title: Predict population-level f0 contours from a GCA fit — predict_gca
text: Builds (time, tone) prediction grid using polynomial basis cached inside fit_gca() result, predicts via lme4 re.form = NA curves reflect population-average fixed effects.

title: Predict population-level f0 contours from a GCA fit — predict_gca
code: predict_gca(gca_obj, n = 100)

title: Predict population-level f0 contours from a GCA fit — predict_gca
text: gca_obj object class "shinytone_gca" returned fit_gca(). n Number time points across [0, 1]. Default 100.

title: Predict population-level f0 contours from a GCA fit — predict_gca
text: data frame columns time, f0_predicted, tone.

what: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: Boots bundled Shiny application ships inside installed package. Equivalent hosted version https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/ running machine, file-size, memory, session limits audio / CSV files never leave computer.

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
code: run_app(...)

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: ... Additional arguments forwarded shiny::runApp() — e.g. port = 4321, launch.browser = FALSE, host = "0.0.0.0".

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
text: return; runs app stopped.

title: Launch the Shinytone app locally — run_app
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   shinytone::run_app()   shinytone::run_app(port = 4321, launch.browser = FALSE) } # }

what: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: interactive Shiny application accompanying R functions citation tone research across tone languages. Integrates full workflow pitch extraction, -speaker f0 normalisation, growth-curve generalised additive mixed modelling, outlier inspection, Chao tone numeral summarisation.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: hosted version app available https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. local launcher (run_app()) added future release UI can served offline package installed.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: Standalone analytical functions (normalise_f0(), fit_gca(), fit_gamm(), contour_to_chao(), ...) extracted Shiny app stages. See NEWS.md current state.

title: shinytone: A Citation Tone Research Hub — shinytone-package
text: Maintainer: Chenzi Xu chenzi.xu@ntu.edu.sg Authors: Chenzi Xu chenzi.xu@ntu.edu.sg Cong Zhang

title: shinytone (development version)
text: First public release R package, alongside existing online Shiny app https://chenzixu.shinyapps.io/shinytone/. Package skin stage: Shiny app continues run unchanged. Standalone analytical functions (normalise_f0(), fit_gca(), fit_gamm(), contour_to_chao(), …) extracted subsequent releases.
